biography:
✨ Sustainability made fun and simple
♻️ Environment | 👫People | Business 🧳

items.caption_text:
Give Plastic and Get Money? 💴 

Mumbai’s Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj International Airport (CSMIA) has installed Reverse Vending Machines (RVM) to encourage people to recycle plastic bottles. 

Basically, if you have an empty plastic bottle you can go and put it in the machine. The machine will then give you swiggy,  myntra coupons.

How cool is that? 

Personally I am not a fan of these coupons. Instead we should give points or cash coupons for donating bottles

Mumbai is following a scheme which is super successful in several european countries like Norway, Germany, Denmark. For example, Norway has had a bottle deposit scheme since 1902 and they continue to run successfully even to this day. In 2021, an average of 91.5 per cent of cans and 92.8 per cent of all purchased plastic bottles were returned in Norway

We are late to the party but these models of reverse vending machines can be a gam changer and must be scaled across India.  To begin with we must give incentives like points/cash to people.. but then when they get used to the system we must charge a small deposit amount for each plastic, glass, metal items so that everyone gets motivated to recycle their waste

What do you think of this recycling scheme? Interesting right?

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainability #sustainabilitytips #reels #reelsinstagram #mumbai #mumbaiairport

items.caption_text:
Eco Tips Shopping Edition 🛍️ 

If your day to day conversation revolves around grocery shopping, then you have officially become an adult 😄

Congratulations 🎉 

But here are some tips I feel that can help you be a bit more sustainable when it comes to grocery shopping:

1. Plan and make a list of items you really want to buy 📝
2. Carry a Reusable Bag 🛍️
3. Eat before going shopping, always helps 🥘 

What are some tips you follow? Do let me know. 

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainability #sustainabilitytips #reels #reelsinstagram #shoppinghacks #shopping

items.caption_text:
Tamil Nadu has become the first state in India to launch climate change missions in all of its 38 districts

What this means is that: every district will now be taking climate change seriously and will be developing and implementing climate change related initiatives to tackle issues specific to those districts

The best part is that, they have already setup a governance council headed by the chief minister and several climate change experts from across the globe to guide and lead this mission. 

Thats a very big step.. to be honest. Usually climate related initiatives are implemented at state level without a dedicated action plan and most times all these initiatives happen after an catastrophe .. like building some form of flood barriers after floods happen etc. So taking action pro-actively is a much needed change in our fight against climate change

So what actions can you expect from the expert council?

As a first step, they will  release data on carbon emissions for each of its departments 

They will also establish a platform (maybe a website or portal) to share all climate change information pertaining to Tamil Nadu State

I am excited to see this become successful and become as a model for all other states in India

What do you think?

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainability #sustainabilitytips #reels #reelsinstagram #climatechange #news

items.caption_text:
Menstrual Leave - Yes or No?

Nowadays there is a lot of debate happening around menstrual leave, is it needed or is it discriminatory? Lot of opinions being shared

But amidst this debate, we are forgetting one important aspect, that is about period poverty

Period Poverty is basically the inability of menstruating population to get access to  menstrual products and basic sanitation facilities. This form of poverty then deprives menstruators from basic opportunities like education, jobs etc.

For Instance in India, approximately 23 million girls drop out of school annually simply because of Period Poverty and many others miss classes on a monthly basis just because they don’t have menstrual products or toilets etc

This is sad, really! 

Government and various NGOs are trying to change this.. by making sanitary napkins and menstrual cups free for school students, going to rural areas and educating the population on hygiene etc

But it’s definitely not simple. Because there are scenarios where some NGO donated free disposable napkins to a village and at first, the menstruators used it. But after few days, they were sceptical. 

You know why?

Menstruators were throwing the used disposable napkin to the near by river, which was their source of drinking water.

So my view is that, we can’t look at such deep problems in a superficial way, we need to go deeper and also change our solutions to meet the needs of the people

What do you think? Whats your opinion on this topic? 

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainability #sustainabilitytips #reels #reelsinstagram #period #periodpoverty

items.caption_text:
Save Mangroves, to Save Mumbai

I repeat “Save Mangroves, to Save Mumbai”

Yes. Mangroves play an important role in protecting mumbai from coastal erosion, storm surges, and flooding. Basically, they act as a natural barrier, absorbing the energy of waves and reducing the impact of storms or sea level rise on the coast.  Somewhat like a sponge or shock absorber! So protecting them is very important 

If you don’t believe it, read about the real-life example of July 2005 Floods in Mumbai, when a record-breaking 944 millimeters of rain hit Mumbai for 24 hours. This flood destroyed thousands of properties, and over 1,000 people lost their lives to the floods. But a fishing village, Bhandup, escaped most of the damage. 

You know why? 

Mangrove forests surrounded this village and cushioned it from floods. 

Yes, it is that effective. 

However, the mangrove ecosystem in Mumbai is under threat from urbanization, waste dumping, and illegal land reclamation. Yes, government is now becoming more aware and have taken steps like setting up a “mangrove conservation cell” 

But it still needs our attention. Therefore, making this video.

So where to start?

As an individual, you can contribute by taking part in mangrove area cleanups and also planting more in nearby area. You can even attend workshops or read more about mangroves. 

There is also an app called “Mangroves App” which can help identify 22 mangrove species in Maharashtra, based on details such as the shape of the leaf or colour. This app is intended to increase interest in mangroves and their importance, and also raise awareness and conservation efforts.

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainability #sustainabilitytips #mangroves #reels #reelsinstagram

items.caption_text:
So France has banned single-use plates, cups, other cutleries for restaurants who serve customers coming to the store to eat food.

To put to context - There are roughly 30,000 fast-food outlets in France serving six billion meals a year. So with this ban an estimated 180,000 tons of waste is going to be reduced. 

You know what’s funny? 

In a way, we are actually going back to old methods of living.  I mean, like before every restaurant was using reusable cutlery’s, glasses and stuff to serve their dine-in customers, right?

But then with an aim to serve more customers, we opted for these throwaway measures.

The reason i am making this video is because In India, we are following the same path like them.. so I feel before its gets out of control, we must stop this throw away habit right here.

We have approximately 3 crores foodservice businesses in India excluding 55,000 hotels (FICCI). Out of this 70Lakh businesses are in the organised sector itself. Yes,  the impact we can create is huge. 

So please lets not take this for granted.. lets opt for reusable options 

However, We shouldn’t put a blanket ban. There must be some thinking, analysis given into how much water, energy use would increase because of the ban. So who is better off, who needs support etc must be studied in detail. Otherwise it will only shift problem from one place to another

Whats your view on this? Do let me know

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainability #reels #reelsinstagram #packagingban

items.caption_text:
Most of us are familiar with air, water, and land pollution, but did you know that light can also cause pollution ?

Hahaha don’t worry its something we can easily tackle

So light pollution is basically caused by inappropriate or excessive use of artificial light. So maybe when an outdoor lighting used at night is inefficient, or overly bright, poorly targeted and, in many cases, completely unnecessary. Also, even these office buildings litt up completely late in the night. I am sure most of ya’ll have seen that?

This light is considered to have some environmental consequences on humans, wildlife, and our climate. Studies have shown its impacting the circadian rhythm of both humans and wildlife. Like in the case of  pigeons, they were found to be confused and started waking up earlier than usual.. (then humans will complain.. birds chirping early is affecting their sleep quality haha) Now further studies are being done to see how this impacts their our world.

Fun fact: According to the 2016 groundbreaking “World Atlas of Artificial Night Sky Brightness,” 80 percent of the world’s population lives under such polluted skies. In a way, everyone of us is getting affected by this.

So what can we do?

Turn off all unnecessary lights to start with and maybe even ask our offices to turn off after everyone leaves

Hope you learnt something new

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainabilitytips #sustainability #reels #reelsinstagram

items.caption_text:
Your “Reusable Bottle” is still a good enough solution to tackle the issue of single use plastic. 

So please don’t panic reading some random research. 

The reason why I am calling this research random is because of the following:

Lack of Credibility: the company which has done this research is not a scientific institution nor has this research been peer-reviewed or published in a credible journal. Its just an article published in the website of a private company founded in the year 2020 , with 3-4 employees (as per their website and LinkedIn) 

Incomplete Methodology: The company has not published any data on how they selected the sample.. what kind of criteria they set for selecting. For example, if they selected sample like a rusted bottle, old bottle or even bottle with normal use.

So we can’t really come to a conclusion that reusable bottles are bad or has more bacteria compared to toilet seat 

Key Takeaway from the Study:

Yes. We must clean our bottles regularly, otherwise they can accumulate a lot of bacteria - just like any other vessel in your household. So just chill and wash your bottles at least once a day! 😄

Hope this post solves the confusion

#sustainability #thesustainabilityguy #reels #reelsinstagram #ecotips #reusablebottles

items.caption_text:
Wondered what happens to your all-natural food/organic waste after you throw it in the dustbin? 

Hmm.. yeah bro - if it’s thrown without segregation, then all of the waste ends up in landfill. Once it reaches here, then it releases a gas called methane – a harmful greenhouse gas that is 25 times more potent than carbon dioxide

So we should stop this habit ok? 

You know something..most times we throw our organic waste in the same bin and don’t feel guilty about it because in our minds we feel these are natural items and there is no reason to associate anything bad with this 

So that’s why governments keep telling us, separate wet waste and dry waste. So when it is separated then, the wet waste goes to the community composter which is now getting installed in most municipalities (If there is no community composter, then you never know)

But the best solution out there is to do the composting ourselves - if you want to learn about it then do follow @wormrani she has already uploaded a lot of videos on this.

Hope this video helps some of you in taking that decision. 

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainabilitytips #sustainability #reels #reelsinstagram #composting #reducereuserecycle

items.caption_text:
Is it Safe to Reuse Plastic Water Bottles?

No, its not recommended 

Most water bottles even has an image asking users to throw the bottle in the bin after single use.

But why?

1. Chemical Leaching: This is when chemicals from the plastic mix with whatever liquid you put inside.. due to sunlight hitting the bottle or wear and tear causing cracks in the bottle 
2. Bacteria Growth: This can happen quickly from the ordinary use.  Even unfinished beverages left outside is seen to have bacteria growth

With these factores in mind, I am sure you are now thinking of going for a reusable bottle 😉 

Source: WebMD 

#sustainability #thesustainabilityguy #reels #reelsinstagram #reducereuserecycle

items.caption_text:
Do you carry your reusable cups when you go to grab your favourite coffee from starbucks?

You carry right? Please tell yes

If you don’t.. then don’t worry

Starbucks actually has got no problem if you carry your cups. Infact they even give you 10 rupee discount if you bring your cup (I asked the malleswaram and brigade road branch in Bengaluru and they confirmed this) 😄

Ok? 

Just because you are reading this, let me also tell you a dirty secret 😄

These disposable coffee cups are not really recyclable. Even though it looks like or feels like paper, these  cups have a  plastic lining in them which makes it hard to recycle. Most brands including starbucks are trying for decades now to make coffee cups recyclable but have not been successful (don’t know if they really want to or not)

But anyways, carrying your reusable cup is the best solution. You get your favourite coffee and also get to reduce your impact

Hope you learnt something new. If you did, like share maaadi 😄

#sustainabilitytips #sustainability #thesustainabilityguy #reels #reelsinstagram #reduceplasticwaste

items.caption_text:
Hyderabad is famous for its biryani, movies and of course all its tourist attractions. 

right? 

But in the future, they will also be famous for developing  India’s first 100 per cent green government building. 

Yes. The State Renewable Energy Development Corporation Limited (TSREDCO) is building a new office in Hyderabad and it is going to be eco friendly 

Like it it will have a Grid- Interactive Net Zero Energy structure spread over 2,591 square metres with a roof garden, wind tower, Building Integrated Photovoltaics (BVIP), recycled timber vertical slabs and measures to control urban flooding in and around the building.  The design also takes into consideration many natural elements so that use of light and ACs is highly reduced 

You know why this is unique ? 

Usually only private sector offices adopt all these green building designs but in this case it’s government. So there are high chances that, once this becomes operational, more such environmental-friendly buildings will come up in large numbers across the country

What do you think?

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainability #ecofriendlybuildings #greenbuildings #sustainabilitytips #reels #reelsinstagram

items.caption_text:
Knock Knock.. 

Whose there?

Your food delivery person! :D

I am sure, everyone of you gets super excited when the food delivery person comes to your doors. If you don’t what even bro :D

But there is one thing which is often not spoken about when it comes to food delivery -that’s the impact on their environment. 

As per the survey conducted in 2018, about 22,000 tonnes of plastic waste was generated by online delivery apps such as Zomato, Swiggy, etc. majorly due to its plastic cutlery. Imagine the overall impact, if we calculate the waste that is being generated  from containers, bowls, cups, lids, foil, sachets, delivery packets, etc.

If you want to do the calculation: 35-40million orders are delivered per month by food delivery companies combined in India

Hugeeeeeee! 

So the least we can do is to reduce online ordering - maybe when we are super tired, then yeah go for it. But once it’s delivered, we must rinse the utensils and store all of the dry waste. Then when after few months, when we collect some good quantity of waste,  we can give it to a recycling facility near by. 

Easy right?

Also, brands must try to change their business model and use reusable containers. Yes, that’s also proven and possible. Startups in the USA like Go Box and DeliverZero for example are using reusable food containers for their food deliveries

What do you think?

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainabilitytips #sustainability #reels #reelsinstagram #endplasticpollution #onlinedelivery

items.caption_text:
India makes USB Type-C charging must for device makers from March 2025

Yes. This is happening (at least as per latest information)

By March 2025, the Bureau of Indian Standards (BIF) will be facilitating the introduction of this universal charger.

This initiative is being implemented with an aim of reducing e-waste. 

Yes. E-wastes are becoming a major environmental concern due to the presence of toxic substances such as lead, mercury and cadmium that can contaminate soil and water sources. In 2020-2021, India generated approximately 3.4 lakh tonnes of e-waste. 

Yes. I know, Chargers produces just a small % of e-wastes but what this law will eventually do is, push the responsibility towards the manufacturers

BTW this is not new, Europe has already brought a similar law.. from 2024, all the portable electronic devices  there will have type C charger.

Anyways what do you think? If you are an iPhone user, then I feel you must be happy. No more running around for chargers 

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainabilitytips #sustainability #reels #reelsinstagram

This initiative is being implemented with an aim of reducing e-waste. 

Yes. E-wastes are becoming a major environmental concern due to the presence of toxic substances such as lead, mercury and cadmium that can contaminate soil and water sources. In 2020-2021, India generated approximately 3.4 lakh tonnes of e-waste.

items.caption_text:
Clothes made from plastic bottles - cool or not?

Ill let you take that guess 😉 

In this video I have tried to highlight the “not so cool” side of the product which no one talks about 

Yes. Whenever clothes are made of plastic bottle.. and when it gets washed….it releases something called as microplastics.. these microplastic like the name suggests are super small in size, so most times we don’t even see its existence

Thats the scary part as well. Because these microplastics are so small, it enters the water bodies like rivers, oceans without any problem

👉 Key takeaways: 

Plastic will still be plastic, be it as a bottle or made in the shape of good looking clothes

Whenever brands come out with some “new” solution to the sustainability problem.. always pause and think about it. If its really sustainable. Because from my experience working in this field for over 5 years, what I have seen and also realised is that - everything related to sustainability has mostly been about getting media attention.. if a can be advertised and brands can benefit from it, then that becomes a solution. 

What do you think?

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainability #reels #reelsinstagram #plasticpollution #endplasticwaste

items.caption_text:
“You know in the future, we will most likely be charged for using electric devices depending on the time of the day

You don’t believe me?

Hahaha it is already a reality in many countries including some parts of India for commercial clients like factories

So there is something called as  time of day (TOD) tariff  which applies variable charges depending on what time of day electricity is used. For example, the price of consuming 1 unit of power will be 10 INR maybe during peak hours (6 a.m. to 10 a.m and 6 p.m. to 9 p.m) compared to say 6 INR during non-peak hours

So this way we can plan our day better and mostly use big equipments like washing machine, grinders during non-peak hours to save on our electricity bills (per unit can save a lot of paisaa 😄) 

Why is the government doing this?

To reduce “peak demand” - so this is a bit technical, I will give a simple example if want to learn about it

During hot summer days, our energy demand for electricity is highest, so keeping this demand in mind, governments build electricity generation plants.. but during say winter months this demand is low. So now what about the electricity generation plants? Will they stay idle? So in order to avoid such unnecessary construction they implement schemes like this to manage the supply and demand 

Same is the case on a day to day basis, during peak hours its highest and then it falls down.. so to manage the power and grid health across the day, such schemes are implemented 

Hope this video is helpful

Also, I am sorry many people checked in on me to see how I am doing and why I am not posting videos. Thank you for that. I am doing good and will slowly start posting some exciting content soon❤️

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainability #reels #reelsinstagram #reducelectricity

items.caption_text:
Everyone knows plastic cups are bad when it comes to its environmental impact..

So most of us now go with paper cups as an alternative to plastic

But do you know even that is not perfect

You know why ?  Disposable paper cups are moslyy made of around 90 to 95% paper, but an additional 5% is made up of a thin plastic coating of polyethelene

This plastic coating is added to make the paper cup waterproof, so that it retains heat and allows it to maintain its shape

and the sad part is when the plastic coating  gets exposed to hot liquids, then it starts to break down, and releases microplastics into your drink

Yes there are other type of paper cups which are made of food grade wax too, but how do we distinguish between the two? Manufacturers also use cheap wax sometimes to reduce their costs, which could then have health impacts

Sad right?

I understand its hard to completely do away with disposable cups, but one can seriously reduce the usage by carrying a reusable bottle everywhere you go. In offices, keep a glass or mug to sip your favourite coffee everytime

Hope this helps

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainability #sustainabilitytips #reelsinstagram #reels

items.caption_text:
Sprite… clear hai?

Coca Cola has announced they are going to change the colour of their flagship Sprite brand from Green to White, to improve the quality of recycling

Ok let me explain how this works: 

See India has one of the highest PET (the type of plastic used to make plastic bottles) recycling rates - nearly 85% of all PET bottles are recycled (Source: TERI, NCL) but much of this recycling (nearly 70%) is recycled by the fibre industry to make clothes, carpets etc. 

Now this is what coca cola is trying to change.. they want to make bottles from recycled materials.  So by changing the colour of their plastic bottles to white, they are claiming they can get good quality plastic pellets, which can be then used to make bottles instead of clothes etc 

Yes, this is true but will it make a big difference to coca cola’s exceptional performance being the world’s top most plastic polluter for 4-5 years in a row?

Fun Fact:  They sell nearly 100 billion plastic bottles a year 😄

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainability #reels #reelsinstagram #sprite #endplasticpollution #cocacola

items.caption_text:
What if someone tells you, you have just 6 years 255 days left to live? How would you react? 

Maybe go to the doctor for solutions right?

What if the doctor then gives you hope and asks you to do certain activities to live more? 

Most of us would do it, right

That’s the situation with climate change.. there is a clock being installed across the globe to demonstrate how quickly our planet is approaching 1.5 °C of global warming, given current emissions trends and scientific evidences. Once the clock reaches this point, then there will be lot of irreversible impacts across the world.. like floods, heatwaves, droughts affecting food, energy security etc. 

BTW the clock is updated every year to reflect the latest global CO2 emissions trend and rate of climate warming. 

Yeah, basically doctor telling us the date and then asking us to take the necessary precautions to tackle climate change. 

What do you think? Did you know of the climate clock? Will this even be taken seriously? Let me know in the comments

#sustainability #thesustainabilityguy #reels #reelsinstagram #climateclock

items.caption_text:
What do you normally use fishing nets for?

To catch a fish right?

Now imagine if that same net and the techniques is used to catch plastic which is in the water before it enters the sea? Cool right? 

Yeah - that’s what Mumbai is doing righy now. 

Few NGOs, think-thanks like Bombay61 Studio (B61), Ministry of Mumbai’s Magic (MMM), research entity Tapestry along with  the “Koli community” came together, designed this entire initiative and conducted a pilot initiative on the banks of versova creek and it was a success. 

They collected 500kg of plastic in just 3 days.  Now they are hoping government takes notice of this and helps them scale it across Mumbai. But I also hope waste is reduced at source, because this solution will  only reduce waste entering the sea, but waste will still be generated. So for that, we have to tackle the root problem

P.S. I love this initiative because its entirely designed by taking into consideration of the local community. I feel this is important because, most times we think western solutions, machines are the “only” way to go ahead. But its not true. We have to listen to the locals, understand their perspective  about the place and challenges, and then use modern technology wherever necessary. 

Hope you like it 

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainabilitytips #sustainability #reelsinstagram #reels #mumbai

items.caption_text:
India is leading the world in the aviation sector when it comes to gender diversity 

According to the International Society of Women Airline Pilots, 12.4% of all pilots in India are women. This is a good number( For comparison, major markets like US and UK have 4-5% itself) 

But how did India reach this level ?

Mainly good policies by airlines: For example, airlines offer a flexible work environment  to women crew members and pilots. Not just that some airlines also offer temperory on ground roles to pregnant women to support them during materinity. This is a big step considering how many women leave work after pregnancy in India

Another major reason behind India’s success story in employing more women is the outreach programs. These programs include awareness regarding job opportunities in the industry. So attending these events allowed women to think about this career option and then pursue it

Because of all this, a level of trust and comfort was built within women. Even their family members (who are often times the barrier) were ok with them pursuing it

But I hope it reflects in  other sectors too, because currently our female labour force participation rates are very low (20-25% compared to 45-50% averages)

But this is definitely a good achievement, what do you think?

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainability #genderdiversity #female #pilots #reels #reelsinstagram

items.caption_text:
Oil and gas industries have 636 representatives at COP 27 (climate conference) – a rise of more than 25% on previous year. 

You know what? This number is only increasing year on year and that is the main problem. Because with every year, their influence on the outcome, policies of climate summits is getting skewed to meet “their” needs, rather than needs of the developing countries or countries getting affected the most due to climate change

I also feel, If a very important and neutral conference like COP becomes a meet and greet event for fossil fuel companies, then its hard to get an optimistic conclusion right?  If not COP, where do we go? 

So I feel, we must not allow this trend to go unnoticed and ask united nations, where they are heading towards. 

What do you think?

#sustainability #thesustainabilityguy #reels #reelsinstagram #ACECC #ACEPartner and #AllEyesOn27 #cop27

items.caption_text:
Good News Alert

Bengaluru airport T2 is getting inaugurated tomorrow. You know what’s so special? The entire terminal is designed with sustainability factors in mind. The theme of the new terminal is that of a “garden” - so there will be lot of plants, gardens, ponds and even a 3 storey forest inside the terminal. The concept aims to give passengers a natural feel of a garden as they pass through every terminal touchpoint

There will also be minimum reliance on artificial illumination, treated water used for the landscape indoor gardens, and this also means that - less dependence on mechanical ACs

Why this is a good step?

Well its true aviation industry is not a big contributor to India’s overall emissions yet (just 1-3%) at the moment and airports contribute to less than 10% of this. So yeah the role of airports is kinda minimal compared to emissions of the airlines and other transport related emissions related to aviation section (90% emissions) 

But that doesn’t mean airports shouldn’t take climate action. Also, this airport can also do what changi airport has done to singapore - inspire a lot of young people people about sustainability and technology etc and become a beacon of hope for the city. This is very much needed particularly in a city like bengaluru.

BTW, this design is also a tribute to revive bengaluru’s identity as the Garden City of India (YES before the Silicon Valley Tag). I really hope this design and identity gets reflected in all the developmental projects that happens here

What’s your view about Bengaluru KIA T2 ?

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainability #thesustainabilityguy #reels #reelsinstagram #bengaluru #bengaluruairport

items.caption_text:
We all love to buy clothes and often times we get confused about which one to buy, right?

We think, clothes that cost lower means its a good deal and vice versa, we think higher price clothes are not affordable. That’s not actually true

Let me explain:

Lets say you are confused between 2 plain tshirts, one costs 500 and other costs 1000. If you are worried about the budget, you would go for the 500 one, thinking its a good deal.  But its not always the case 

So in such situations, its always good to estimate how many times you expect to wear the tshirt before buying it

Scenario 1: Black tshirt which you expect to wear 5 times or which will last you 5 times

Cost (Black) = 500
Expected to use (Black) = 10 times
Cost per use =500/10= 100 rupees per use

Scenario 2: white tshirt which you expect to wear 20 times or which will last you for 20 times

Cost (White) = 1000
Expected to use (White) = 20
Cost per use = 1000/20 = 50 rupees per use

So this way in reality scenario 2 is cheaper for you per use. 

Got it?

#thesustainabilityguy #reelsinstagram #reels #sustainabilitytips #sustainability #reelsindia

items.caption_text:
China is going all-in on Panda’s, I mean Panda shaper Solar Power Plants

So china has developed a 250-acre solar farm in the shape of a panda. The effort is partially a PR campaign to attract chinese youth towards clean energy and also to showcase its might to the global audience that they are leading the world in renewable energy investment. 

You may wonder why Panda?

Well, thats another interesting story on how china uses Panda (the animal) as its soft diplomacy power across the world. Its very interesting. My brother @lordmoneyengar has beautifully explained it in one of his recent reels. Do check it out 

Anyways do you think India should do these PR activities, but what shape should we go for ? Or chup chaap kaam karo is the way to go? 

#thesustainabilityguy #reelsinstagram #sustainabilitytips #reels #reelsinstagram

items.caption_text:
We can’t deny - we are all facing huge impacts (floods, heatwaves, droughts etc) due to climate change 

Almost everyday there is a media headline of some place within India or outside in chaos - all of this is bad, particularly for developing countries.

Why? See our per capita is low, we have so many millions of people living on daily wages and also in poverty. So during extreme weather events, they are the first in line to face the brunt (Check videos of Pakistan, what happened to their people) 

But the sad part is that - developing countries have not caused this problem and expecting them to go “all-in” on environment when there is so more important social complexities, is not fair in any ways. 

Developed countries, like the U.S and those in the European Union, are responsible for most of the heat-trapping emissions. So they must compensate poorer ones for the “loss and damage” caused by climate change.

Next week at the COP27 summit, this is going to be a big agenda point (I’ll update you guys about it) 

Can India and other developing countries get them to pay for it? Lets wait and watch

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainability #sustainabilitytips #reels #reelsinstagram

But the sad part is that - developing countries have not caused this problem and expecting them to go “all-in” on environment when there is so more important social complexities, is not fair in any ways. 

Developed countries, like the U.S and those in the European Union, are responsible for most of the heat-trapping emissions. So they must compensate poorer ones for the “loss and damage” caused by climate change.

Next week at the COP27 summit, this is going to be a big agenda point (I’ll update you guys about it) 

Can India and other developing countries get them to pay for it? Lets wait and watch

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainability #sustainabilitytips #reels #reelsinstagram

items.caption_text:
“Ek letter ki kheemat tum kya janoge ramesh babu?”

Letter?

Yes. Letter is a very powerful tool which can be used to raise your concerns, particularly if the company which you are working is not doing anything for causes like climate change. You can simply write an open letter collectively among your peers (ofcourse in a polite tone) stating why causes like climate change must be supported by the company and why its an important issue for you as an individual/group. 

But what actions can you demand on an immediate basis?

Well, In my current company - we demanded actions ranging from banning single-use plastic, installing waste segregation bins, turning off lights after office hours, changes to HVAC systems, training and incentives to help people make better environmental choices. 

But then there are many strategic requests you can make as well - like reducing emissions to net zero and also first calculating how much of current business that comes from fossil fuel and high emitting companies, then slowly arriving at a consensus to reduce business relationships with such clients. 

What do you think? Have you had such conversations at your workplace? If not, what is stopping you? Do let me know in the comments

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainability #sustainabilitytips #reels #reelsinstagram #letter

items.caption_text:
We’ve all been there before: Just last week I was checking for a nice shoes online and the discounts were crazy. The minute I saw the discounts on the product. I put it in my cart. No questions asked. 

Luckily I don’t keep my card linked to my account and  the card which I use for online transaction, I keep in my cupboard drawer and I was lazy to complete the full transaction. Anyways I planned to buy this shoes later. 

After sometime my mind changed - I started asking myself do I really need it, is it better to buy something else etc. Unintentionally I was following the 24 hours rule- under this rule, you basically the delay the completion of the transaction, so if you like a product, you put it in your cart and wait for 24 hours and then take a call if you really want or not (ofcourse I didn’t buy it yet - still it irks me every now and then). So I thought let me make a reel only on this topic 😄

If you go through such mad implulsive reactions, lol then you are not alone.  24 hours rule - works okishly for me, sometimes the cravings takes over.

What methods do you follow to stop this? 

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainability #reels #reelsinstagram #impulseshopping #impulsebuy #business

items.caption_text:
Tree planting is a nobel act, no doubt. But we must also give equal importance to preserving the existing trees

Why am I talking about this?

Well, if you clearly observe, thesedays we see a lot of trees stuck or nailed with advertisement posters of big companies - maybe because this is like free advertisement and no one really cares about the legality

But this is really bad for the trees, as it creates holes that give access to termites and insects.  Often times, advertisement companies also inject the trees with mercury and sulphuric acid. This way slowly the tree becomes weak. Once a tree becomes weak, then you know right - companies wait like a hawk to cut it

Sad

But do you know doing all this illegal (like most things) and you can actually file a complaint with the local municipal body. Otherwise, you can also click pictures and ring those companies and request them to remove it or you can also hold them responsible on social media - that also works

What do you think? Have you seen such trees in your local viscinity? 

Lets see if we can make a difference 

Source: NDTV Swachh India 

#thesustainabilityguy #sustainability #reelsinstagram #reels #trees
